the main clause is negative in meaning (”rarely attends”), the tag is positive (”does she”).

questions.question: Match the words in List I with their meanings in List II List I List II A. Cite I. to quote words from a book B. Petrol II. place where a building is situated C. Site III. Fuel is liquid used for vehicles D. Bite IV . to go around checking for safety
questions.explanation: ”Cite” means to quote (I). ”Petrol” is fuel (III). ”Site” refers to a location (II). ”Bite” could refer to going around checking for safety (in the sense of a police dog, for instance). (IV).

questions.question: Rearrange the jumbled words to form a meaningful sentence: A. displacements from B. further lens C. large-scale 23 D. special E. economic zones
questions.explanation: The correct order forms the sentence: ”Large-scale displacements from special economic zones further lens.” While the sentence might sound a bit unusual, it implies that mass displacements due to economic zones are being observed or analyzed (using ”lens” metaphorically to represent focus or perspective). Grammatical Structure: The phrase “further lens” is likely a metaphor suggesting that these displacements provide additional insight or perspective on economic zones, even though the wording seems unconventional.

questions.question: Choose the word that is opposite in meaning to ”SPURIOUS”:
questions.explanation: ”Spurious” refers to something false, counterfeit, or lacking authenticity. The antonym of spurious is ”genuine,” meaning authentic or real. Context: Understanding opposites like ”spurious” (false) and ”genuine” (real) helps in enhancing vocabulary and distinguishing between different types of objects or information.

questions.question: The food served for lunch was INSIPID. Rewrite the sentence by giving the antonym to the underlined word.
questions.explanation: ”Insipid” means lacking flavor, dull, or unappetizing. The appropriate antonym is ”delicious,” which describes food that has a pleasing and enjoyable taste. Context: ”Insipid” is often used to describe bland or boring food, while ”delicious” is used to highlight something flavorful and enjoyable.

questions.question: Match the blanks in List I with the correct Adverbs in List II List I List II A. He will pay for his mistake. I. ever B. I do not remember having stories before. II. dearly C. I think that Ravi succeeded in scoring a century III. scarcely D. does anyone believe in ghost these days. IV . barely
questions.explanation: The correct matching is: • A. He will pay ”dearly” (a high price) for his mistake (A-II). • B. ”Scarcely” means barely or hardly (B-III). • C. Ravi succeeded ”barely” (C-IV), implying that his success was not easily achieved. • D. ”Barely” anyone believes in ghosts these days (D-IV), emphasizing the rarity of belief. Context of Adverbs: Adverbs such as ”barely” and ”scarcely” express limitations or rarity, whereas ”dearly” emphasizes the intensity or value of an action.

questions.question: Sanjeev stayed at work he could complete the pending work.
questions.explanation: ”So that” is used to indicate purpose. In this sentence, it shows the reason for Sanjeev staying at work — to complete his pending work. Context of ”So That”: ”So that” is commonly used to explain the purpose or result of an action, such as working to achieve a goal.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one that suits the best as a question tag for the sentence: She rarely attends the class, ?
questions.explanation: The correct question tag is ”does she?” Question tags use the auxiliary verb (or ”do/does/did” if no auxiliary is present) and the pronoun from the main clause, and they are opposite in polarity (positive sentence, negative tag; negative sentence, positive tag). Since the main clause is negative in meaning (”rarely attends”), the tag is positive (”does she”). Context of Question Tags: Question tags are used to confirm or check information. When the sentence is negative, the tag is positive, and vice versa.

questions.question: Rearrange the words in the correct order to form a meaningful sentence: A. ever B. happens C. by D. chance E. nothing
questions.explanation: The correct sentence is ”Nothing ever happens by chance,” meaning that events are not random or accidental. Grammar Tip: In English, the word order is crucial to making sentences meaningful. Adverbs like ”ever” typically come before the verb, and prepositional phrases like ”by chance” usually follow the verb.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the phrase ’A feather in one’s cap’?
options: something that you have achieved and are proud of
something that is fragile and thus important to you
something that exists in colours and makes you stand out.
something that makes you feel soft and delicate.
questions.explanation: ”A feather in one’s cap” is an idiom meaning an achievement that one is proud of, something that adds to one’s prestige or reputation. Context of Idioms: The phrase ”a feather in one’s cap” comes from the practice of warriors adding a feather to their cap as a symbol of their victories.

questions.question: Read the text below and identify the options that shows the use of an idiomatic expression: 28 I went to stay with my cousin last month. We are of the same age but have very little in common. He loves sports and I hate it.
questions.explanation: ”Have very little in common” is an idiom meaning to share few interests or characteristics. Context of Idioms: Idioms often express abstract ideas in more concrete terms. In this case, ”have very little in common” conveys the idea of minimal shared interests.

questions.question: Choose the option that serves as an Adjective Clause in the sentence: The boy, whom you see bowling, is my cousin.
questions.explanation: ”Whom you see bowling” is the adjective clause, modifying the noun ”boy” by providing more information about him. Context of Adjective Clauses: Adjective clauses provide more detail about a noun or pronoun in a sentence. They usually begin with a relative pronoun (who, whom, whose, that, which).

questions.question: Rearrange the given statements in proper chronological sequence in an ascending order (earliest to latest): (A) General theory of Employment, Interest and Money by Keynes. (B) Mahalanobis was made a fellow of Britain’s Royal Society. (C) An Inquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith. (D) The Economic Consequences of the Peace by Keynes. Choose from the options given below:
questions.explanation: - (C) Adam Smith’s ”Wealth of Nations” was published in 1776. - (D) Keynes’s ”The Economic Consequences of the Peace” was published in 1919. - (A) Keynes’s ”General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money” was published in 1936. - (B) Mahalanobis was made a fellow of Britain’s Royal Society in 1945.

questions.question: Rearrange the given statements in proper chronological sequence in an ascending or- der (earliest to latest): (A) General theory of Employment, Interest and Money by Keynes. (B) Mahalanobis was made a fellow of Britain’s Royal Society. (C) An Inquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith. (D) The Economic Consequences of the Peace by Keynes. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: - (C): Adam Smith’s *Wealth of Nations* was published in 1776. - (D): Keynes’s *Economic Consequences of the Peace* was published in 1919. - (A): Keynes’s *General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money* was published in 1936. - (B): Mahalanobis became a fellow of Britain’s Royal Society in 1945.

questions.question: Rearrange the given statements in proper chronological sequence in ascending 20 order (earliest to latest): (A) General Theory of Employment, Interest, and Money by Keynes. (B) Mahalanobis was made a fellow of Britain’s Royal Society. (C) An Inquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith. (D) The Economic Consequences of the Peace by Keynes. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: - (C) Smith’s Wealth of Nations: Published in 1776. - (D) Keynes’s Economic Consequences: 1919. - (A) Keynes’s General Theory: 1936. - (B) Mahalanobis became Royal Society Fellow: 1945.

questions.question: Rearrange the given statements in proper chronological sequence in an ascend- ing order (earliest to latest): (A) General Theory of Employment, Interest, and Money by Keynes. (B) Mahalanobis was made a fellow of Britain’s Royal Society. (C) An Inquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith. (D) The Economic Consequences of the Peace by Keynes. 15 Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: Chronological Order: - (C) Wealth of Nations (Adam Smith, 1776). - (D) Economic Consequences of Peace (Keynes, 1919). - (A) General Theory (Keynes, 1936). - (B) Mahalanobis Fellow (1945).

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct option. The teacher believed that the student’s sudden lack of interest in class was an ..........., as he had always been enthusiastic and attentive.
questions.explanation: The sentence suggests that the student’s behavior was out of character. The word anomaly means something unusual or unexpected, which fits perfectly in this context. It indicates that the student’s behavior was a deviation from the norm. - Option (1) is incorrect because incentive refers to something that motivates or encourages an action, which doesn’t fit the meaning of the sentence. - Option (3) is incorrect because proverb means a short, commonly known saying—not related to describing a behavior. - Option (4) is incorrect because alleviation refers to relief or reduction of something unpleasant, which doesn’t describe an unusual behavioral pattern. Answer: The correct answer is option (2): anomaly.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct option. Despite the chaos during the emergency, the nurse remained ........... and performed her duties with great precision.
questions.explanation: The sentence describes a situation of emergency and chaos, but the nurse is calm and focused. The word placid means calm and composed, making it the most suitable choice. - Option (2) erratic means unpredictable or inconsistent—this contradicts the idea of calmness. - Option (3) callous means emotionally insensitive or unfeeling, which does not describe calm behavior in a crisis. - Option (4) giddy means light-headed or dizzy, often associated with silliness, not composure. Answer: The correct answer is option (1): placid.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct option. The manager’s decision to cut staff was met with .......... from the employees, who felt it was unfair and poorly timed.
questions.explanation: The employees reacted negatively to the staff cuts, indicating anger or bitterness. The word resentment perfectly captures this emotional response. - Option (1) elation means great happiness, which contradicts the context. 3 - Option (2) gratitude means thankfulness, which is inappropriate given the negative situation. - Option (4) admiration implies respect and approval, which is unlikely if employees thought the decision was unfair. Answer: The correct answer is option (3): resentment.

questions.question: Re-arrange the following parts of a sentence in their correct sequence to form a meaningful sentence. (A) is crucial for maintaining good health (B) and getting enough sleep every night (C) following a balanced diet (D) regular exercise, Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: This sentence lists habits that contribute to maintaining good health. The logical structure should begin with the subject (D) “regular exercise,” followed by other health habits (C and B), and finally the result or main clause (A). The correct rearranged sentence reads: ”Regular exercise, following a balanced diet and getting enough sleep every night is crucial for maintaining good health.” - (D) introduces the subject. - (C) and (B) describe additional habits. 4 - (A) concludes the sentence with the main point. Other sequences either break the subject-verb agreement or create awkward phrasing. Answer: The correct answer is option (4): (D), (C), (B), (A).

questions.question: Re-arrange the following parts of a sentence in their correct sequence to form a meaningful sentence. (A) the decision was announced publicly (B) after weeks of speculation and media reports (C) by the government officials (D) during a press conference Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: The sentence should start with who made the announcement — (C) “by the government officials” — followed by when or where it occurred — (D) “during a press conference.” The main action — (A) “the decision was announced publicly” — comes next, and the context — (B) “after weeks of speculation and media reports” — completes the sentence. The properly ordered sentence is: ”By the government officials, during a press conference, the decision was announced publicly after weeks of speculation and media reports.” - The logical subject-agent flow is preserved. - Time and cause-based context (B) comes at the end. - It reads naturally and formally, which is expected in government-related statements. 5 Answer: The correct answer is option (4): (C), (D), (A), (B).

questions.question:
Choose the word that best fits the blank in the sentence below: ”Despite his remarks,
the audience found his ideas intriguing and thought-provoking.”
questions.explanation:
The sentence contrasts his remarks with the audience’s reaction. This implies that
his remarks might have been negative, yet his ideas were interesting. ”Derisive” means
mocking or contemptuous, which fits this contrast.
Explanation of other options: - ”Laudatory” means expressing praise — inconsistent with
contrast. - ”Euphemistic” refers to mild or indirect language. - ”Succinct” means brief and to
the point — does not express contrast.

questions.question: Identify the grammatically correct sentence:
options: Neither the professor nor the students was able to attend the lecture.
Neither the professor nor the students were able to attend the lecture.
Neither the professor or the students was able to attend the lecture.
Neither the professor nor the students is able to attend the lecture.
questions.explanation:
When using ”Neither...nor...”, the verb agrees with the noun closest to it. Here,
”students” is plural, so the correct verb form is ”were”. ”Neither...nor...” is also correct —
”or” is grammatically wrong in this structure.

questions.question:
What literary device is used in the line: ”The silence was so loud, it echoed in her
chest”?
questions.explanation:
This line exaggerates silence by saying it was ”so loud” — a clear example of
hyperbole, or exaggerated statement for effect.
- Oxymoron involves two contradictory words (e.g., ”deafening silence”). - Metonymy is a
figure of speech where a thing is called by something closely associated. - Synecdoche uses
a part to represent the whole or vice versa.

questions.question: Choose the word closest in meaning to ”inscrutable”:
questions.explanation:
”Inscrutable” means something difficult to understand or interpret. The synonym
that best matches this is ”mysterious”.
- ”Transparent” means easy to perceive or detect. - ”Predictable” means expected or
foreseeable. - ”Eloquent” refers to fluent or persuasive speaking.

questions.question:
The professor’s explanation was so esoteric that even graduate students struggled to
understand it. What is the meaning of ”esoteric” in this context?
options: Intended for or likely to be understood by a small, specialized group
questions.explanation:
”Esoteric” refers to something that is intended for or understood by only a small,
specific group with specialized knowledge. The sentence suggests that even graduate
students found it difficult, which aligns with this meaning.

questions.question: Identify the sentence with the correct modifier placement:
options: Running down the street, my backpack fell off.
I almost saw 30 birds in the sky.
She served sandwiches to the children on paper plates.
Exhausted after the meeting, the report was finally written by Ravi.
questions.explanation:
This sentence clearly indicates that the children received sandwiches served on
paper plates. Other sentences contain misplaced modifiers: - (1) suggests the backpack was
running. - (2) suggests the speaker ”almost saw” the birds. - (4) suggests the report was
exhausted.

questions.question:
Identify the literary device in the sentence: ”Time crawled as she waited for the
results.”
questions.explanation:
The sentence gives human-like behavior (”crawled”) to time, which is not human.
This is a classic case of personification.
- Simile uses “like” or “as”. - Metaphor makes a direct comparison. - Irony involves a
contrast between expectation and reality.

questions.question: Choose the word that is opposite in meaning to ”superfluous”:
questions.explanation:
”Superfluous” means unnecessary or excessive. The opposite of this is
”necessary”.
- ”Excessive” and ”redundant” are synonyms, not antonyms. - ”Decorative” is unrelated in
meaning.

questions.question:
Rearrange the parts to form a coherent sentence:
A) is no longer a privilege
B) that only the elite enjoy
C) quality education
D) in the modern world
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the core subject and idea
The phrase “quality education” (C) is the subject. It is then qualified by the context “in the
modern world” (D).
Step 2: Add the main verb phrase
Next, “is no longer a privilege” (A) follows naturally after C-D.
Step 3: Add the relative clause
Lastly, “that only the elite enjoy” (B) provides further information about the previous clause.
Final sentence:
”Quality education in the modern world is no longer a privilege that only the elite enjoy.”

questions.question:
Rearrange the parts to form a coherent sentence:
A) when it is no longer fun.
6
B) stop doing something
C) if you’re not growing
D) or learning from it
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the main clause
The phrase “stop doing something” (B) acts as the main imperative.
Step 2: Add the condition
Followed by “if you’re not growing” (C) and “or learning from it” (D), which form the
condition.
Step 3: Add the time clause
Finally, “when it is no longer fun.” (A) explains the circumstance.
Final sentence:
”Stop doing something if you’re not growing or learning from it when it is no longer fun.”

questions.question:
Although the scientist was initially met with skepticism, her theory was eventually
the cornerstone of modern physics and became a .
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Understanding the sentence
The sentence states that the scientist’s theory was initially met with skepticism but later
became very important.
Step 2: Analyze options
- repudiated means rejected — opposite of what is intended here.
- validated means confirmed or accepted — fits the context.
- circumvented means avoided — unrelated here.
- contradicted means opposed — opposite of what fits.
Conclusion:
The correct word is ”validated,” meaning the theory was accepted.

questions.question: Had he the importance of the meeting, he would have made sure to attend.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Sentence structure
The sentence uses a conditional phrase starting with ”Had he,” which is a subjunctive form.
Step 2: Correct verb form
After ”Had he,” the past participle ”known” is required to form the past perfect subjunctive.
Step 3: Verify other options
- ”knew” is simple past, incorrect here.
- ”knows” is present tense, incorrect.

- ”knowing” is gerund/participle, incorrect.
Conclusion:
The correct form is ”Had he known.”

questions.question:
The company’s profits dropped significantly this quarter; , it plans to expand
into three new markets.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Understand sentence meaning
The first part shows a negative event (profits dropped), the second part is a positive plan
(expansion).
Step 2: Analyze connectors
- consequently: implies result, usually negative after a negative, so incorrect.
- meanwhile: implies simultaneous action, unrelated here.
- nevertheless: means despite that, fits the contrast.
- similarly: implies similarity, incorrect here.
Conclusion:
”Nevertheless” correctly shows contrast.

questions.question:
Her autobiography is filled with reflections on failure, giving readers a rare
glimpse into her vulnerabilities.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Analyze meaning
The autobiography offers a ”rare glimpse” into vulnerabilities, so reflections are honest.
Step 2: Analyze options
- superficial means shallow, opposite of deep insight.
- candid means honest and straightforward, fits well.
- deceptive means misleading, opposite meaning.
- indifferent means unconcerned, unrelated here.
Conclusion:
”Candid” best fits the context.

questions.question:
Fill in the blank with the correct option.
The teacher believed that the student’s sudden lack of interest in class was an ..........., as he
had always been enthusiastic and attentive.
questions.explanation:
The sentence suggests that the student’s behavior was out of character. The word anomaly
means something unusual or unexpected, which fits perfectly in this context. It indicates that
the student’s behavior was a deviation from the norm.
- Option (1) is incorrect because incentive refers to something that motivates or encourages
an action, which doesn’t fit the meaning of the sentence.
- Option (3) is incorrect because proverb means a short, commonly known saying—not
related to describing a behavior.
- Option (4) is incorrect because alleviation refers to relief or reduction of something
unpleasant, which doesn’t describe an unusual behavioral pattern.
Answer: The correct answer is option (2): anomaly.

questions.question:
Fill in the blank with the correct option.
Despite the chaos during the emergency, the nurse remained ........... and performed her
duties with great precision.
questions.explanation:
The sentence describes a situation of emergency and chaos, but the nurse is calm and
focused. The word placid means calm and composed, making it the most suitable choice.
- Option (2) erratic means unpredictable or inconsistent—this contradicts the idea of
calmness.
- Option (3) callous means emotionally insensitive or unfeeling, which does not describe
calm behavior in a crisis.
- Option (4) giddy means light-headed or dizzy, often associated with silliness, not
composure.
Answer: The correct answer is option (1): placid.

questions.question:
Fill in the blank with the correct option.
The manager’s decision to cut staff was met with .......... from the employees, who felt it was
unfair and poorly timed.
questions.explanation:
The employees reacted negatively to the staff cuts, indicating anger or bitterness. The word
resentment perfectly captures this emotional response.
- Option (1) elation means great happiness, which contradicts the context.

- Option (2) gratitude means thankfulness, which is inappropriate given the negative
situation.
- Option (4) admiration implies respect and approval, which is unlikely if employees thought
the decision was unfair.
Answer: The correct answer is option (3): resentment.

questions.question:
Re-arrange the following parts of a sentence in their correct sequence to form a
meaningful sentence.
options: is crucial for maintaining good health
and getting enough sleep every night
questions.explanation:
This sentence lists habits that contribute to maintaining good health. The logical structure
should begin with the subject (D) “regular exercise,” followed by other health habits (C and
B), and finally the result or main clause (A).
The correct rearranged sentence reads: ”Regular exercise, following a balanced diet and
getting enough sleep every night is crucial for maintaining good health.”
- (D) introduces the subject.
- (C) and (B) describe additional habits.

- (A) concludes the sentence with the main point.
Other sequences either break the subject-verb agreement or create awkward phrasing.
Answer: The correct answer is option (4): (D), (C), (B), (A).

questions.question:
Rearrange the following parts to form a meaningful sentence:
A. many health experts have raised concerns
B. not just about its physical impacts
C. about the rapid spread of the new virus
D. but also about its effects on mental well-being
questions.explanation:
The sentence starts with C: ”about the rapid spread of the new virus,” followed by A: ”many
health experts have raised concerns”. Then it adds B: ”not just about its physical impacts”
and finally D: ”but also about its effects on mental well-being”. This order forms a coherent
and meaningful sentence.
Answer: CABD

questions.question:
Rearrange the following parts to form a meaningful sentence:
A. with the rise of digital platforms and online learning,
B. students must develop strong self-discipline
C. to manage their time effectively
D. and stay motivated without constant supervision
questions.explanation:
The sentence logically begins with the introductory phrase A: ”with the rise of digital
platforms and online learning,” followed by the main clause B: ”students must develop
strong self-discipline”. The purpose is explained by C: ”to manage their time effectively”
and then an additional related action D: ”and stay motivated without constant supervision”.
This order forms a smooth, meaningful sentence.
Answer: ABCD

questions.question:
Rearrange the following parts to form a meaningful sentence:
A. the author explores the complexities of human relationships
B. through the lens of a young protagonist
C. who navigates a world filled with emotional conflict
D. and an unspoken longing for connection
questions.explanation:
The sentence starts with A: ”the author explores the complexities of human relationships,”
followed by B: ”through the lens of a young protagonist.” Then it explains more about the

protagonist using C: ”who navigates a world filled with emotional conflict,” and finally adds
D: ”and an unspoken longing for connection.” This order makes a meaningful and complete
sentence.
Answer: ABCD

questions.question:
Despite his repeated failures, he remained and continued working towards his
goals.
questions.explanation:
The sentence describes someone who, despite many failures, continues to work towards their
goals. ”Persistent” means continuing firmly despite difficulties, which fits perfectly. -
”Arrogant” means having an exaggerated sense of one’s own importance, which does not fit
here. - ”Indifferent” means showing no interest or concern, which contradicts the
continuation of efforts. - ”Hesitant” means uncertain or slow to act, which conflicts with
continued working. Thus, ”persistent” is the best fit.
Answer: persistent

questions.question: The manager asked the team to complete the project the end of the week.
questions.explanation:
The correct preposition for a deadline is ”by,” meaning on or before a specific time. - ”Until”
implies continuing up to a point, but does not express deadline completion. - ”Since” is used
to indicate the starting point of an action or event. - ”For” indicates duration. Hence, ”by the
end of the week” is the correct phrase indicating the project deadline.
Answer: by

questions.question:
Her speech was so that the entire audience sat in silence, deeply moved by her
words.
questions.explanation:
The clue is ”the entire audience sat in silence, deeply moved,” indicating the speech had a
strong impact. - ”Powerful” means having great effect or influence, which fits well. -
”Persuasive” means convincing, but it doesn’t necessarily cause silence and deep emotion. -

”Dramatic” relates to theatrics, not necessarily deep emotion. - ”Emotional” is close, but
”powerful” better captures the overall effect. Hence, ”powerful” best completes the sentence.
Answer: powerful

questions.question: The scientist was awarded for her contribution to renewable energy research.
questions.explanation:
The scientist was awarded for her contribution, which must be notable. - ”Innovative” means
introducing new ideas or methods, which fits here. - ”Temporary” means lasting for a short
time, irrelevant. - ”Careless” is negative and inappropriate. - ”Optional” means not
compulsory, irrelevant. Therefore, ”innovative” is the correct choice.
Answer: innovative

questions.question: If the train is delayed, we will have to our travel plans accordingly.
questions.explanation:
The sentence suggests changing plans due to delay. - ”Adjust” means to modify or change to
suit new conditions, which fits perfectly. - ”Abandon” means to give up entirely, stronger
than needed here. - ”Cancel” means to call off plans, which is possible but less flexible. -
”Oppose” means to resist or go against, which is illogical in this context. Hence, ”adjust” is
the best choice.
Answer: adjust

questions.question: Choose the word that is similar in meaning to: ”Eliminate”
questions.explanation:
”Eliminate” means to completely remove or get rid of something. - ”Remove” is a synonym
as it means to take away. - ”Create” is the opposite, meaning to bring into existence. -
”Increase” means to make something larger or more. - ”Ignore” means to pay no attention,
which is not synonymous. Thus, ”Remove” is the correct choice.
Answer: Remove

questions.question:
The judge was known for being impartial in all her rulings. Choose the word that is
closest in meaning to impartial:
questions.explanation:
”Impartial” means not supporting any side, fair, and unbiased. - ”Neutral” means not taking
sides, which is synonymous with impartial. - ”Biased” means favoring one side unfairly. -
”Hostile” means unfriendly or antagonistic. - ”Arrogant” means having an exaggerated sense
of self-importance. Hence, ”Neutral” is the correct answer.
Answer: Neutral

questions.question:
The manager tried to mitigate the tension between the two teams. Choose the word
that is opposite in meaning to mitigate:
questions.explanation:
”Mitigate” means to make something less severe or serious. - The opposite would be
”Intensify,” meaning to increase in strength or severity. - ”Lessen” is a synonym, meaning to

reduce. - ”Avoid” means to stay away from. - ”Settle” means to resolve. Therefore,
”Intensify” is the correct opposite.
Answer: Intensify

questions.question:
When Rahul said he could complete the project in two days, I took it with a grain of
salt. What does the idiom ”with a grain of salt” mean?
questions.explanation:
The idiom ”take something with a grain of salt” means to view it with skepticism or doubt. -
It does not mean complete belief (A) or outright ignoring (B). - It implies cautious
acceptance, i.e., accepting but doubting. - Being surprised (D) is unrelated. Hence, option
(C) is correct.
Answer: Accepted it but with doubt

questions.question:
Sentence: After losing the match, the coach told the team to keep their chin up.
What does the idiom ”keep your chin up” mean?
options: Remain cheerful in a difficult situation
questions.explanation:
The idiom ”keep your chin up” means to stay positive and hopeful, especially during tough
or disappointing times. - It does not mean to be proud and arrogant (A). - It is different from
being ready to fight (B) or focused on winning (D). - The correct meaning is to remain
cheerful or optimistic even after setbacks.
Answer: Remain cheerful in a difficult situation

questions.question:
Sentence: The new policy is just a flash in the pan; it won’t last long. What does the
phrase ”flash in the pan” mean?
questions.explanation:
The phrase ”flash in the pan” originally referred to the flintlock firearms which sometimes
produced a flash but failed to fire. Now, it means something that shows initial promise or
success but fails to last or have a lasting impact. - It is not a permanent solution (A). - It is
not a sudden failure (B), but rather a success that is short-lived. - It is not a long-term
achievement (D). Thus, the phrase means a brief success (C).

Answer: A brief success

questions.question:
Rearrange the following parts to form a meaningful sentence:
A) in the hall
B) to attend the seminar
C) the students gathered
D) early in the morning
questions.explanation:
Let’s analyze the sentence parts: - ”the students gathered” (C) is likely the subject and verb
starting the sentence. - ”early in the morning” (D) gives the time of gathering. - ”in the hall”
(A) tells the place where they gathered. - ”to attend the seminar” (B) explains the purpose of
gathering.
Putting these together in the order:
C (the students gathered)+D (early in the morning)+A (in the hall)+B (to attend the seminar)
forms the meaningful sentence: ”The students gathered early in the morning in the hall to
attend the seminar.”
Answer: CDAB

questions.question:
Rearrange the following parts to form a meaningful sentence: A) the match
B) before the rain started
C) they finished
D) just in time
questions.explanation:
Let’s analyze the parts to form a coherent sentence: - ”they finished” (C) is the subject and
verb, starting the sentence. - ”the match” (A) is the object of the verb ”finished.” - ”just in
time” (D) indicates the timing of finishing. - ”before the rain started” (B) gives additional
time context.
Arranging these parts as C A D B gives the sentence: ”They finished the match just in time
before the rain started. ”
This sequence makes a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence.
Answer: CADB

questions.question:
Choose the option that best completes the sentence:
“Hardly had the train arrived the passengers rushed to board.”
questions.explanation:
The sentence uses the inversion structure “Hardly had...” which is typically followed by
“when” to indicate that one event occurs immediately after another. Here, “Hardly had the
train arrived when the passengers rushed to board” means the passengers rushed as soon as
the train arrived. “Then” is used for sequential actions without inversion, “after” implies a
delay, and “that” is grammatically incorrect in this context. Thus, the correct answer is:
when

questions.question:
Identify the synonym of the word ‘abstain’ as used in the sentence:
“Doctors advise people to abstain from smoking.”
questions.explanation:
In the sentence, “abstain” means to refrain or hold back from doing something, specifically
smoking. Among the options, “avoid” means to keep away from or not engage in, which
closely matches the meaning of “abstain.” “Indulge” means to partake freely, the opposite of
abstain. “Support” implies endorsing, and “delay” suggests postponing, neither of which fit.
Thus, the correct answer is:
Avoid

questions.question: Find the correctly punctuated sentence:
options: My sister who lives in Mumbai is coming to visit.
My sister, who lives in Mumbai, is coming to visit.
My sister who lives in Mumbai, is coming to visit.
My sister, who lives in Mumbai is coming to visit.
questions.explanation:
The phrase “who lives in Mumbai” is a non-restrictive relative clause, providing additional
information about “my sister” and requiring commas to separate it from the main clause.
Option (B) correctly places commas before and after the clause: “My sister, who lives in
Mumbai, is coming to visit.” Option (A) lacks commas, making it restrictive (implying
multiple sisters). Option (C) has an incorrect comma placement, and (D) misses a comma
after “Mumbai.” Thus, the correct answer is:
My sister, who lives in Mumbai, is coming to visit.

questions.question:
Rearrange the following sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful
paragraph:
(P) He started to explore the forest nearby.
(Q) Rahul was fond of adventures.
(R) One day, he found a strange cave.
(S) He lived on the edge of a large town.
questions.explanation:
To form a coherent paragraph, start with (S), which sets the context by describing where
Rahul lived. Next, (Q) introduces Rahul’s character as fond of adventures, providing
motivation. Then, (P) describes his action of exploring the forest, a logical outcome of his
adventurous nature. Finally, (R) concludes with the event of finding a strange cave. The
sequence S, Q, P, R forms a logical narrative:
S: He lived on the edge of a large town. Q: Rahul was fond of adventures. P: He started to
explore the forest nearby. R: One day, he found a strange cave.
Thus, the correct order is:
S, Q, P, R

questions.question: Select the word closest in meaning to ‘Benevolent’:
questions.explanation:
The word “benevolent” means showing kindness or goodwill, often in a charitable sense.
Among the options, “kind” directly aligns with this meaning, as it denotes being friendly and
considerate. “Cruel” is an antonym, meaning harsh or unkind. “Wealthy” relates to riches,
and “cunning” implies deceit, neither of which match “benevolent.” Thus, the correct answer
is:
Kind

questions.question:
Complete the sentence:
“The proposal was accepted reluctance.”
questions.explanation:
The sentence requires a preposition that appropriately describes the manner in which the
proposal was accepted. “Reluctance” is a noun indicating unwillingness or hesitation. The
preposition “with” is commonly used to express the manner or attitude accompanying an
action, as in “accepted with reluctance,” meaning the acceptance was hesitant. “At” and “in”

are typically used for time or place, and “by” indicates agency or means, none of which fit
the context. Thus, the correct answer is:
with

questions.question: Choose the correct meaning of the idiom: “To turn over a new leaf”:
questions.explanation:
The idiom “to turn over a new leaf” means to make a fresh start or improve one’s behavior or
habits for the better. Among the options, “to change for the better” directly captures this
meaning. “To plant a garden” is literal and unrelated, “to write a book” does not reflect the
idiom’s meaning, and “to change one’s job” is too specific. Thus, the correct answer is:
To change for the better

questions.question:
7
Arrange the following sentences to form a coherent paragraph:
(P) They can learn a lot from their mistakes.
(Q) Students should not be afraid of making mistakes.
(R) Mistakes are often the best teachers.
(S) Accepting errors positively is a sign of growth.
questions.explanation:
To form a coherent paragraph, start with (Q), which introduces the main idea that students
should not fear mistakes. Next, (R) explains why, stating that mistakes are the best teachers,
providing a reason. Then, (S) elaborates that accepting errors positively indicates growth,
building on the idea of learning from mistakes. Finally, (P) concludes by reinforcing that
students can learn a lot from their mistakes, completing the logical flow. The sequence Q, R,
S, P forms a coherent paragraph:
Q: Students should not be afraid of making mistakes. R: Mistakes are often the best
teachers. S: Accepting errors positively is a sign of growth. P: They can learn a lot from
their mistakes.
Thus, the correct order is:
Q, R, S, P

questions.question:
Select the word closest in meaning to ‘Impeccable’:
8
questions.explanation:
The word “impeccable” means without fault or error, indicating perfection. Among the
options, “flawless” directly aligns with this meaning, as it also denotes being perfect or
without defects. “Ordinary” suggests something common, “doubtful” implies uncertainty,
and “dull” means lacking interest, none of which match “impeccable.” Thus, the correct
answer is:
Flawless

questions.question: Select the word closest in meaning to ‘Candid’:
questions.explanation:
The word “candid” means being truthful, straightforward, or frank in expression. Among the
options, “honest” closely aligns with this meaning, as it denotes truthfulness and sincerity.
“Deceptive” implies misleading, an antonym of candid. “Reserved” suggests being

restrained or shy, and “hesitant” indicates indecision, neither of which match “candid.” Thus,
the correct answer is:
Honest

questions.question: Select the word most nearly opposite in meaning to ‘Obscure’:
questions.explanation:
The word “obscure” means unclear, hidden, or difficult to understand. An antonym would
mean easily understood or visible. Among the options, “clear” directly opposes “obscure” by
indicating clarity or transparency. “Hidden” and “mysterious” are synonyms, reinforcing
obscurity, and “vague” is similar in meaning to obscure. Thus, the correct answer is:
Clear

questions.question:

“Her approach to solving problems impressed her colleagues.”
questions.explanation:
The sentence suggests that the approach led to a positive outcome, as it impressed
colleagues. “Methodical” means systematic and organized, fitting the context of an
impressive problem-solving approach. “Hasty” implies rushing and potential errors,
“careless” suggests negligence, and “reluctant” indicates unwillingness, none of which align
with impressing others. Thus, the correct answer is:
Methodical

questions.question:
Complete the sentence:
“The team faced the challenge confidence, leading to their success.”
questions.explanation:
The sentence requires a preposition to describe how the team faced the challenge, leading to
success. “With” is used to indicate the manner or attitude accompanying an action, as in
“faced with confidence,” meaning confidently. “By” suggests agency or means, “in” is used
for states or locations, and “at” typically denotes a point or target, none of which fit as well.
Thus, the correct answer is:
with

questions.question:
Arrange the following sentences to form a coherent paragraph:
(P) She began practicing yoga to improve her health.
(Q) Over time, she became a certified yoga instructor.
(R) Anjali was inspired by her friend’s fitness journey.
(S) Her dedication led to significant physical and mental benefits.
questions.explanation:
To form a coherent paragraph, start with (R), which introduces Anjali’s inspiration from her
friend’s fitness journey, setting the context. Next, (P) describes her action of practicing yoga
to improve her health, a direct response to the inspiration. Then, (S) outlines the benefits of
her dedication, showing the outcome. Finally, (Q) concludes with her achievement of
becoming a certified yoga instructor. The sequence R, P, S, Q forms a logical narrative:

R: Anjali was inspired by her friend’s fitness journey.P: She began practicing yoga to
improve her health. S: Her dedication led to significant physical and mental benefits. Q:
Over time, she became a certified yoga instructor.
Thus, the correct order is:
R, P, S, Q

questions.question:
Arrange the following sentences to form a coherent paragraph:
(P) It provides access to vast amounts of information instantly.
(Q) The rise of the internet has transformed education.
(R) Students can now learn from online courses and resources.
(S) However, it requires responsible use to avoid distractions.
questions.explanation:
To form a coherent paragraph, start with (Q), which introduces the main idea of the internet’s
impact on education. Next, (P) elaborates by explaining how it provides instant access to
information. Then, (R) specifies how students benefit through online courses and resources.
Finally, (S) concludes with a caution about responsible use to avoid distractions, using
“however” to signal a shift. The sequence Q, P, R, S forms a coherent paragraph:
Q: The rise of the internet has transformed education. P: It provides access to vast amounts
of information instantly. R: Students can now learn from online courses and resources. S:

However, it requires responsible use to avoid distractions.
Thus, the correct order is:
Q, P, R, S

questions.question: Complete the sentence: “The team faced the challenge confidence, leading to their success.”

Thus, the correct answer is: with

questions.question: Arrange the following sentences to form a coherent paragraph: (P) She began practicing yoga to improve her health. (Q) Over time, she became a certified yoga instructor. (R) Anjali was inspired by her friend’s fitness journey. (S) Her dedication led to significant physical and mental benefits.
questions.explanation: To form a coherent paragraph, start with (R), which introduces Anjali’s inspiration from her friend’s fitness journey, setting the context. Next, (P) describes her action of practicing yoga to improve her health, a direct response to the inspiration. Then, (S) outlines the benefits of her dedication, showing the outcome. Finally, (Q) concludes with her achievement of becoming a certified yoga instructor. The sequence R, P, S, Q forms a logical narrative: 12 R: Anjali was inspired by her friend’s fitness journey.P: She began practicing yoga to improve her health. S: Her dedication led to significant physical and mental benefits. Q: Over time, she became a certified yoga instructor. Thus, the correct order is: R, P, S, Q

questions.question: Arrange the following sentences to form a coherent paragraph: (P) It provides access to vast amounts of information instantly. (Q) The rise of the internet has transformed education. (R) Students can now learn from online courses and resources. (S) However, it requires responsible use to avoid distractions.
questions.explanation: To form a coherent paragraph, start with (Q), which introduces the main idea of the internet’s impact on education. Next, (P) elaborates by explaining how it provides instant access to information. Then, (R) specifies how students benefit through online courses and resources. Finally, (S) concludes with a caution about responsible use to avoid distractions, using “however” to signal a shift. The sequence Q, P, R, S forms a coherent paragraph: Q: The rise of the internet has transformed education. P: It provides access to vast amounts of information instantly. R: Students can now learn from online courses and resources. S: 13 However, it requires responsible use to avoid distractions. Thus, the correct order is: Q, P, R, S

questions.question: In the sentence “Her response was so equivocal that no one could ascertain her true intentions,” the word ”equivocal” most nearly means:

(A) ”Clear and concise” is the opposite of ”equivocal.” (C) ”Emotionally charged” refers to strong emotions, which is not implied in the sentence. questions.question: All poets are dreamers. Some dreamers are not realists. Therefore, which of the following must be true?
questions.explanation: The two premises are: 1. All poets are dreamers. (i.e., Poets ⊆ Dreamers) 2. Some dreamers are not realists. 2 Let’s analyze the options: (A) “Some poets are not realists”: Not necessarily true. Poets are dreamers, and some dreamers are not realists, but we don’t know if those specific dreamers include poets. (B) “All dreamers are poets”: This reverses the first statement. We know all poets are dreamers, but not all dreamers are poets. (C) “Some realists are not poets”: We have no information about the set of realists in relation to poets. Therefore, none of the options (A), (B), or (C) must be true.

questions.question: Rearrange the following parts to form a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence: P. to innovate continuously Q. is what sets R. a successful company S. apart from its competitors
questions.explanation: Step 1: Identify the subject and verb. The subject is clearly “a successful company” (R), and the verb phrase “is what sets” (Q) follows it. This makes “RQ” a logical beginning. Step 2: Identify the remaining meaningful connectors. What sets the company apart is its ability “to innovate continuously” (P), and it is being compared with “its competitors” (S). So the logical order is: R → Q → S → P Step 3: Combine for full sentence. “A successful company is what sets apart from its competitors to innovate continuously.” — This is grammatically incorrect. Correction: Actually, correct sequence is: “A successful company is what sets it apart from its competitors: to innovate continuously.” So correct order is: RQSP
